title: std::vector — the dynamic array
instruction:
`vector` grows automatically. It's the C++ equivalent of Python's list or Java's ArrayList.

```cpp
#include <vector>
vector<int> nums = {1, 2, 3};
nums.push_back(4);  // adds to the end
nums.size();        // 4
```

Run this.
successMessage: .back() returns the last element. push_back adds to the end, pop_back removes it. Unlike Java's ArrayList, vector doesn't box values — int stays int, no overhead.

title: Iterate a vector
instruction:
Three ways to iterate a vector:
1. Classic index for loop — when you need the index
2. Range-based for — cleaner, when you just need values
3. Iterator — the traditional C++ way

All produce the same result — range-based for is the modern choice.
successMessage: size_t for the index — it's the unsigned type returned by .size(). Comparing int i < fruits.size() causes a signed/unsigned warning. Use size_t or just use range-based for.

title: Vector methods
instruction:
Key vector operations:

- `.push_back(val)` — add to end
- `.pop_back()` — remove from end
- `.insert(pos, val)` — insert at position
- `.erase(pos)` — remove at position
- `.clear()` — empty the vector
- `.empty()` — check if empty
- `.front()` / `.back()` — first/last element
successMessage: v.begin() is an iterator to the first element. v.begin() + 2 points to the third. insert and erase use iterators — you'll see this pattern constantly in C++ algorithms.

title: C arrays and std::array
instruction:
C arrays are the original — fixed size, no bounds checking, legacy.
`std::array<T, N>` is C++11's fixed-size array with safety and STL compatibility.

Prefer `std::array` over C arrays for fixed-size needs.
successMessage: .at(2) throws out_of_range on bad index; [2] doesn't. std::array has .size(), works with range-for, and is just as fast as C arrays. Use it whenever you know the size at compile time.

title: Find min, max, sum in a vector
instruction: Calculate statistics by iterating. The `<algorithm>` header has `min_element`, `max_element`, and `accumulate` from `<numeric>`.
hint: *min_element(v.begin(), v.end()) gives the minimum value (dereference the iterator).
successMessage: The * before min_element dereferences the iterator to get the value. accumulate sums from 0. These STL algorithms work on any range — vectors, arrays, any container.

title: 2D vector — matrix
instruction: A `vector<vector<T>>` creates a 2D structure — like a matrix or grid:
successMessage: vector<vector<int>>(3, vector<int>(3, 0)) creates a 3x3 grid initialized to 0. 2D vectors power game boards, image processing, and mathematical matrices.

title: Build a grade analyser
instruction:
No starter code.

Given `vector<int> grades = {72, 88, 95, 64, 81, 77, 90, 55, 83, 69}`:

1. Print sorted grades (use `sort` from `<algorithm>`)
2. Print min and max
3. Print average to 1 decimal
4. Count passing (>= 60) and print `"PASS: X/10"`
hint: sort(grades.begin(), grades.end()); for sorting. Use accumulate for sum.

codeChecks.message: Use sort() from <algorithm> to sort grades.
Find and print the minimum grade.

successMessage: A complete grade analyser with sorting, statistics, and counting. These patterns — sort, min/max, accumulate — are the building blocks of every data-crunching program. Chapter 8 done!
